items.q: Did the Gestapo have agents watching every German?
items.correct: No - the Gestapo was a relatively small organisation that depended heavily on tip-offs and denunciations from ordinary Germans.
items.misconception: The Gestapo had an agent on every street corner watching everyone.
items.random_wrong: The Gestapo was the German army's tank division.
items.why: The terror worked partly because people policed each other; recognising the role of denunciation shows how the regime really controlled society.

items.q: Did all young Germans enthusiastically support the Hitler Youth?
items.correct: No - membership became effectively compulsory by 1939, and groups such as the Edelweiss Pirates and Swing Youth rejected its discipline and culture.
items.misconception: Every young German was a devoted Nazi through the Hitler Youth.
items.random_wrong: The Hitler Youth was banned by the Nazis in 1936.
items.why: Compulsory membership is mistaken for enthusiasm; pockets of youth nonconformity are key evidence about the limits of Nazi control.

items.q: Could ordinary Germans have been unaware that Jews were being persecuted?
items.correct: No - the 1933 boycott, the Nuremberg Laws and Kristallnacht were public and publicised, although knowledge of the wartime death camps varied.
items.misconception: Ordinary Germans could not have known about any persecution of the Jews.
items.random_wrong: Persecution of the Jews only began after the war started in 1939.
items.why: Students conflate the secrecy around extermination with the very public pre-war persecution that happened in front of everyone.

items.q: What did the Nuremberg Laws of 1935 do?
items.correct: Stripped Jews of citizenship and banned marriage between Jews and other Germans
items.misconception: Banned Jews from emigrating from Germany
items.random_wrong: Set up the first concentration camp at Dachau
items.why: Correct answer: Stripped Jews of citizenship and banned marriage between Jews and other Germans. “Banned Jews from emigrating from Germany” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the role of the Gestapo?
items.correct: A secret state police that investigated opposition, relying heavily on public denunciations
items.why: It's A secret state police that investigated opposition, relying heavily on public denunciations. “Hitler's personal bodyguard regiment” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which organisation did the Nazis use to indoctrinate boys?
items.why: It's The Hitler Youth. “The Edelweiss Pirates” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What happened on Kristallnacht in November 1938?
items.correct: Nazi-organised attacks destroyed synagogues and Jewish shops, and around 30,000 Jewish men were sent to camps
items.misconception: Jewish citizenship was restored by court order
items.random_wrong: The first Allied bombing raid hit Berlin
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nazi-organised attacks destroyed synagogues and Jewish shops, and around 30,000 Jewish men were sent to camps. “Jewish citizenship was restored by court order” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year were the Nuremberg Laws passed?
items.why: The real figure is 1935 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
